code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of building‑related triples into a fluent, natural‑language sentence.
    """
    # Mapping from predicate to a readable phrase (more concise for common predicates)
    verb_map = {
        "architecturalStyle": "has an architectural style of",
        "buildingStartDate": "was started in",
        "completionDate": "was completed in",
        "floorCount": "has",
        "location": "is located in",
        "cost": "costs",
        "floorArea": "covers a floor area of",
        "architect": "was designed by",
        "owner": "is owned by",
        "formerName": "was formerly known as",
        "height": "has a height of",
        "buildingType": "is a",
        "developer": "was developed by",
        "tenant": "has a tenant",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "country": "is located in the",
        "currentTenants": "currently houses",
        "address": "has the address",
        "inaugurationDate": "was inaugurated on",
        "birthPlace": "was born in",
        "deathPlace": "died in",
        "bedCount": "has",
        "region": "is in the region of",
        "state": "is in the state of",
        "website": "has website",
        "yearOfConstruction": "was constructed in",
        "NationalRegisterOfHistoricPlacesReferenceNumber": "has NRHP reference number",
        "addedToTheNationalRegisterOfHistoricPlaces": "was added to the NRHP on",
        "significantBuilding": "is known for the building",
        "governingBody": "is governed by",
        "leader": "leader is",
        "chancellor": "has chancellor",
        "governmentType": "has government type",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "language": "speaks",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "currency": "uses currency",
        "ethnicGroup": "has ethnic group",
        "origin": "originates from",
        "significantProject": "is known for the project",
        "foundationPlace": "was founded in",
        "demonym": "has demonym",
        "numberOfRooms": "has",
        "keyPerson": "has key person",
        "architecture": "features architecture",
        "postalCode": "postal code is",
    }

    # Group triples by subject and generate a single fluent sentence per subject,
    # using relative clauses (whose, that) for most predicates to reduce redundancy.
    from collections import defaultdict
    subject_groups = defaultdict(list)
    for t in triples:
        subject_groups[t.subject].append(t)

    # Mapping predicates that are naturally expressed as "whose <predicate> is <object>"
    rel_predicates = {
        "leader": "leader",
        "currency": "currency",
        "capital": "capital",
        "language": "language",
        "postalCode": "postal code",
        "architect": "architect",
        "height": "height",
        "buildingStartDate": "construction started",
        "inaugurationDate": "inauguration date",
        "origin": "origin",
        "demonym": "demonym",
        "ethnicGroup": "ethnic group",
        "significantBuilding": "significant building",
        "significantProject": "significant project",
        "founder": "founder",
        "foundingDate": "founding date",
    }

    def clean_obj(o: str) -> str:
        return o.strip('"').strip("'")

    sentences = []
    for subject, group in subject_groups.items():
        # collect predicates
        pred_objs = {}
        for tr in group:
            pred_objs.setdefault(tr.predicate, []).append(tr.object)

        # Start building the main clause
        # Prefer a location predicate to place the subject first with its location
        main_clause = ""
        if "location" in pred_objs:
            loc = clean_obj(pred_objs["location"][0])
            main_clause = f"The {subject} is located in {loc}"
            handled = {"location"}
        else:
            main_clause = f"{subject}"
            handled = set()

        # Add relative clauses for predicates that fit the "whose ..." pattern
        rel_clauses = []
        for pred, objs in pred_objs.items():
            if pred in handled:
                continue
            if pred in rel_predicates:
                phrase = rel_predicates[pred]
                obj_str = " and ".join(clean_obj(o) for o in objs)
                # special wording for some predicates
                if pred == "height":
                    # ensure unit
                    if not any(unit in obj_str.lower() for unit in ["metre", "meter", "ft", "feet"]):
                        obj_str = f"{obj_str} metres"
                rel_clauses.append(f"whose {phrase} is {obj_str}")
                handled.add(pred)
        # Append any remaining predicates using a generic verb phrase
        remaining_clauses = []
        for pred, objs in pred_objs.items():
            if pred in handled:
                continue
            verb = verb_map.get(pred, "has")
            obj_str = ", ".join(clean_obj(o) for o in objs)
            remaining_clauses.append(f"{verb} {obj_str}")

        # Combine all parts
        all_parts = [main_clause] + rel_clauses + remaining_clauses
        sentence = ", ".join(all_parts) + "."
        sentences.append(sentence)

    final_text = " ".join(sentences)
    if final_text:
        final_text = final_text[0].upper() + final_text[1:]
    return final_text

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END
